title: The data make a clear case against Wall Street's "Sell in May" cliche
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: The "Sell in May" Wall Street cliche is historically inaccurate; recent data shows stocks often rise in May and beyond. In 2026, this pattern holds true, defyin
excerpt: The "Sell in May" Wall Street cliche is historically inaccurate; recent data shows stocks often rise in May and beyond. In 2026, this pattern holds true, defying the old adage.

content.introduction: The "Sell in May" Wall Street cliche is historically inaccurate; recent data shows stocks often rise in May and beyond. In 2026, this pattern holds true, defying the old adage.
body: The data make a clear case against Wall Street's "Sell in May" cliche. Phil Rosen 1h. Apple is betting that hardware will win the AI era. Phil

The S&P 500 enters is weakest seasonality stretch of the year. **Good morning, investors.** It doesn’t feel like it, but the S&P 500 has gained almost 28% over the last 12 months, geopolitical uncertainty and all. A red-hot S&P 500 doesn’t squash the “sell in May” cliche that follows markets every year. May is the fourth-weakest month of the year historically, and it also kicks off a stretch of seasonality so middling that it’s earned itself a moniker. **In nine of the past 10 years** stocks have gained during this stretch, and 2025 brought the strongest “sell in May” cycle on record, according to Ryan Detrick, chief market strategist for Carson. As *Opening Bell Daily* has documented, stocks continue to smash records despite the Iran conflict, elevated oil prices, rising inflation fears and a changing of the guard at the Federal Reserve. * Positive in May 12 of the last 13 years.
Sell in May 2026 isn't about summer. It's about midterm election years, and the data going back to 1930 proves the adage only works one year
keyTakeaways: The "Sell in May" Wall Street cliche is historically inaccurate; recent data shows stocks often rise in May and beyond.
